revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:فوجيوارا (عشيرة)]][[be:Фудзівара (род)]][[br:Klann Fujiwara]][[ca:Clan Fujiwara]][[cs:Fudžiwara]][[de:Fujiwara (Familie)]][[el:Αδελφότητα Φούτζιγουαρα]][[en:Fujiwara clan]][[es:Clan Fujiwara]][[eu:Fujiwara klana]][[fa:خاندان فوجیوارا]][[fi:Fujiwara-klaani]][[fr:Clan Fujiwara]][[he:בית פוג'יווארה]][[hr:Fujiwara (klan)]][[hu:Fudzsivara család]][[id:Klan Fujiwara]][[it:Clan Fujiwara]][[ja:藤原氏]][[ko:후지와라씨]][[la:Domus Fujiwara]][[ms:Suku Fujiwara]][[nl:Fujiwara (familie)]][[pl:Ród Fujiwara]][[pt:Clã Fujiwara]][[ru:Фудзивара (род)]][[sh:Fujiwara (klan)]][[sk:Fudžiwara]][[sr:Фуџивара клан]][[sv:Fujiwara]][[ta:புஜிவாரா குலம்]][[th:ตระกูลฟูจิวาระ]][[tr:Fujiwara boyu]][[uk:Рід Фудзівара]][[vi:Gia tộc Fujiwara]][[zh-classical:藤原氏]][[zh-min-nan:Fujiwara--sī]][[zh:藤原氏]]
{{short description|Powerful family of regents in Japan}}{{Japanese clan name| |surname = Fujiwara |surname nihongo = 藤原 |image =File:Japanese_crest_Sagari_Fuji.svg |caption = [[:en:Mon (badge)|''Mon'']]: ''Sagarifuji'' |province of origin ={{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} |parent house =[[Nakatomi clan]] of [[Ame-no-Koyane]] of legendary genealogy [[Izanagi]] |founder ={{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|en|Fujiwara no Kamatari|qid=Q433995|lt=Fujiwara no Kamatari|1=Fujiwara no Kamatari}} |founding year =668 |cadet branches =* [[Nanke (Fujiwara)|Nanke Family]] * [[:en:Hokke (Fujiwara)|Hokke Family]] * [[:en:Shikike|Shikike Family]] * [[:en:Kyōke|Kyōke Family]]
}} The {{nihongo|'''Fujiwara clan'''|藤原氏|Fujiwara-shi {{lang|en|or|italics=no}} Fujiwara-[[Uji (clan)|uji]]}} was a powerful family of imperial {{ill|Regent|en|Regent|qid=Q477406|lt=Regent|1=Regent}}s in Japan, descending from the [[Nakatomi clan]] and, as legend held, through them their ancestral god [[Ame-no-Koyane]]. The Fujiwara prospered since the ancient times and dominated the {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|en|Imperial Court in Kyoto|qid=Q11680900|lt=imperial court|1=Imperial Court in Kyoto}} until the [[Meiji Restoration]] in 1868. They held the title of [[Ason]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Encyclopedia Maipedia|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|isbn=9784582096316|location=Japan|at=Fujiwara-uji}}</ref> The abbreviated form is {{nihongo|'''Tōshi'''|藤氏}}.<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2005). "Fujiwara" at {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', pp. 200-201|page=201}}.</ref>
The 8th century clan history ''Tōshi Kaden'' (藤氏家伝) states the following at the biography of the clan's patriarch, {{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|en|Fujiwara no Kamatari|qid=Q433995|lt=Fujiwara no Kamatari|1=Fujiwara no Kamatari}} (614–669): "Kamatari, the Inner Palace Minister who was also called ‘Chūrō'',''’ was a man of the Takechi district of Yamato Province. His forebears descended from Ame no Koyane no Mikoto; for generations they had administered the rites for Heaven and Earth, harmonizing the space between men and the gods. Therefore, it was ordered their clan was to be called Ōnakatomi"<ref>{{Cite book|last=Bauer|first=Mikael|title=The History of the Fujiwara House}}</ref>
The clan originated when the founder, {{ill|Fujiwara no Kamatari|en|Fujiwara no Kamatari|qid=Q433995|lt=Nakatomi no Kamatari|1=Fujiwara no Kamatari}} (614–669) of the [[Nakatomi clan]], was rewarded by {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|qid=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}} with the honorific "Fujiwara" —after the [[wisteria]] (藤 ''fuji'') field on [[:ja:多武峰|Mount Tōno]] (in present day {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|qid=Q127026|lt=Sakurai City|1=Sakurai, Nara}}) where Kamatari and the then-Prince Naka, who he befriended in a game of {{ill|Kemari|en|Kemari|qid=Q12224003|lt=Kemari|1=Kemari}}, and conspired to {{ill|Isshi incident|en|Isshi incident|qid=Q1062771|lt=eliminate the Soga clan|1=Isshi incident}}—<ref>{{cite book |last1=Reischauer |first1=Edwin O. |last2=Yamagiwa |first2=Jospeh K. |author1-link=Edwin Reischauer |author2-link=Joseph K. Yamagiwa |title=Translations from Early Japanese Literature |date=1951 |publisher=Harvard University Press |page=347}}</ref> which evolved as a surname for Kamatari and his descendants.{{efn|Technically speaking, the name Fujiwara was {{nihongo|''[[kabane]]''|姓|extra=relationship with Emperor}}, not {{nihongo|''kamyō''|家名||extra=lit. "family name"}} nor {{nihongo|''Myōji''|苗字||lit. "surname"}}. In other words, there was no ''[[kuge]]'' (court noble) family called Fujiwara but a group of nobles who shared the same ''kabane'' Fujiwara. For more about names in Japan, see {{ill|Japanese name|en|Japanese name|qid=Q877834|lt=Japanese name|1=Japanese name}}.}} In time, Fujiwara became known as a clan name.<ref>Plutschow, Herbert E. (1995). ''Japan's Name Culture: the Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context.'' pp. 49, 97.</ref>
The Fujiwara dominated the Japanese politics of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} (794–1185) through the monopoly of regent positions, [[Sesshō and Kampaku|''Sesshō'' and ''Kampaku'']].<ref>Nussbaum, ''Fujiwara no Kamatari'' at {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|p. 203|page=203}}.</ref> The family's primary strategy for central influence was through the marrying of Fujiwara daughters to {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=the Emperors|1=Emperor of Japan}}. Through this, the Fujiwara would gain influence over the next emperor who would, according to family tradition of that time, be raised in the household of his mother's side and owe loyalty to his grandfather.<ref>[http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/221568/Fujiwara-Family Britannica: Fujiwara Family] accessed 28/4/2012</ref> As abdicated emperors took over power by exercising ''{{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|qid=Q865416|lt=insei|1=Cloistered rule}}'' ({{nihongo2|院政}}, cloistered rule) at the end of the 11th century, then followed by the rise of the {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=warrior class|1=Samurai}}, the Fujiwara gradually lost its control over mainstream politics.
The {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|en|Northern Fujiwara|qid=Q1050942|lt=Northern Fujiwara|1=Northern Fujiwara}} (Ōshū Fujiwara) ruled the {{ill|Tōhoku region|en|Tōhoku region|qid=Q129465|lt=Tōhoku region|1=Tōhoku region}} (northeast [[Honshu|Honshū]]) of Japan during the 12th century.<ref name=aaa>{{Cite book|author=LOUIS FREDERIC |chapter=O Japão |title=Dicionário e Civilização |location=Rio de Janeiro |publisher=Globo Livros |year=2008 |pages=223–224 |isbn=9788525046161 | chapter-url= https://books.google.com/books?id=pKqDbBSLuSoC&q=Fujiwara+no+Yasuhira&pg=PA293}}</ref>
Beyond the 12th century, they continued to monopolize the titles of ''[[Sesshō and Kampaku|Sesshō]]'' and ''[[Sesshō and Kampaku|Kampaku]]'' for much of the time until the system was abolished in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}. Though their influence declined, the clan remained close advisors to the succeeding Emperors.
== Asuka and Nara period == The Fujiwara clan's political influence was initiated during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}}. Nakatomi no Kamatari, a member of the lower-nobility [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi family]] led a {{ill|Coup d'état|en|Coup d'état|qid=Q45382|lt=coup|1=Coup d'état}} against the [[Soga clan|Soga]] in 645 and initiated a series of sweeping government [[:en:Reform movement|reforms]] that would be known as the [[Taika Reform]]. In 668 Emperor Tenji (reigned 668–671), bestowed the ''[[kabane]]'' {{nihongo|Fujiwara no [[Ason]]|藤原朝臣}} on Kamatari. The surname passed to the descendants of {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|en|Fujiwara no Fuhito|qid=Q177517|lt=Fujiwara no Fuhito|1=Fujiwara no Fuhito}} (659–720), the second son and heir of Kamatari, who was prominent at the court of several emperors and empresses during the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}. He made his daughter Miyako a concubine of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|qid=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu|1=Emperor Monmu}}. Her son, Prince Obito became {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|qid=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}}. Fuhito succeeded in making another of his daughters, {{ill|Empress Kōmyō|en|Empress Kōmyō|qid=Q837932|lt=Kōmyōshi|1=Empress Kōmyō}}, the empress consort of Emperor Shōmu. She was the first empress consort of Japan who was not a daughter of the imperial family itself. Fuhito had four sons; and each of them became the progenitor of a cadet branch of the clan: * the [[:en:Hokke (Fujiwara)|Hokke]] or Northern branch founded by {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|en|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|qid=Q2173602|lt=Fujiwara no Fusasaki|1=Fujiwara no Fusasaki}}<ref>Nussbaum, "Fujiwara no Fusasaki" at {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|p. 202|page=202}}.</ref> * the [[:en:Kyōke|Kyōke]] branch founded by {{ill|Fujiwara no Maro|en|Fujiwara no Maro|qid=Q426317|lt=Fujiwara no Maro|1=Fujiwara no Maro}}<ref>Nussbaum, "Fujiwara no Maro" at {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|p. 205|page=205}}.</ref> * the [[Nanke (Fujiwara)|Nanke]] or Southern branch founded by {{ill|Fujiwara no Muchimaro|en|Fujiwara no Muchimaro|qid=Q1890878|lt=Fujiwara no Muchimaro|1=Fujiwara no Muchimaro}}<ref>Nussbaum, "Fujiwara no Muchimaro" at {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|p. 207|page=207}}.</ref> * the [[:en:Shikike|Shikike]] branch founded by {{ill|Fujiwara no Umakai|en|Fujiwara no Umakai|qid=Q1370638|lt=Fujiwara no Umakai|1=Fujiwara no Umakai}}<ref>Nussbaum, "Fujiwara no Umakai" at {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|p. 211|page=211}}.</ref> Among them, the Hokke came to be considered as the leaders of the entire clan. All four brothers died in 737 during a {{ill|735–737 Japanese smallpox epidemic|en|735–737 Japanese smallpox epidemic|qid=Q60752789|lt=major smallpox epidemic in Japan|1=735–737 Japanese smallpox epidemic}}.<ref name=farris59>{{Cite book| last = Farris| first = William Wayne | title = Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900 | publisher = Harvard University Asia Center | year = 1985 | page = 59 | isbn = 9780674690059}}</ref>
== Heian period == {{Main|Heian period}}
During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} of Japanese history, the [[:en:Hokke (Fujiwara)|Hokke]] managed to establish a hereditary claim to the position of {{ill|Regent|en|Regent|qid=Q477406|lt=Regent|1=Regent}}, either for an underage emperor (''[[Sesshō and Kampaku|sesshō]]'') or for an adult one (''[[:en:Kampaku|Kampaku]]''). Some prominent Fujiwaras occupied these positions more than once, and for more than one emperor. Lesser members of the Fujiwara were [[:en:Noble court|court]] [[:en:Japanese nobility|nobles]], {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|qid=Q860290|lt=provincial|1=Provinces of Japan}} {{ill|Governor|en|Governor|qid=Q132050|lt=Governor|1=Governor}}s and vice governors, members of the provincial {{ill|Aristocracy|en|Aristocracy|qid=Q123432|lt=Aristocracy|1=Aristocracy}}, and {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}}. The Fujiwara was one of the four great families that dominated Japanese politics during the Heian Period (794 AC–1185 AC), and the most important of them at that time. The others were {{ill|Tachibana clan (kuge)|en|Tachibana clan (kuge)|qid=Q1500519|lt=the Tachibana|1=Tachibana clan (kuge)}}, [[Taira clan|the Taira]] and [[Minamoto clan|the Minamoto]]. The Fujiwara exercised tremendous power, especially during the period of regency governments in the 10th and 11th centuries, having many emperors as practically {{ill|Puppet monarch|en|Puppet monarch|qid=Q7260716|lt=Puppet monarch|1=Puppet monarch}}s.
The Fujiwara dominated the government of Japan 794–1160. There is no clear starting point of their dominance. However, their domination of civil administration was lost by the establishment of the first shogunate (i.e., {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|qid=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}) under [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] in 1192.
Fujiwara princes initially served as highest ministers of the imperial Court (''kampaku'') and regents (''sesshō'') for underage monarchs. The Fujiwara were the proverbial "power behind the throne" for centuries. Apparently they never aspired to supplant the imperial dynasty. Instead, the clan's influence stemmed from its matrimonial alliances with the imperial family. Because consorts of crown princes, younger sons, and emperors were generally Fujiwara women, the male heads of the Fujiwara house were often the father-in-law, brother-in-law, uncle, or maternal grandfather of the emperor. The family reached the peak of its power under {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|en|Fujiwara no Michinaga|qid=Q433133|lt=Fujiwara no Michinaga|1=Fujiwara no Michinaga}} (966–1027). He was the grandfather of three emperors, the father of six empresses or imperial consorts, and the grandfather of seven additional imperial consorts; it is no exaggeration to say that it was Michinaga who ruled Japan during this period, not the titular Emperors. As a result of these unusually strong familial links, Michinaga never took the title of Kampaku—he held more than the power that the position would bring, and had no need of the title.
The Fujiwara clan is featured prominently in ''{{ill|The Pillow Book|en|The Pillow Book|qid=Q615137|lt=The Pillow Book|1=The Pillow Book}}'', by {{ill|Sei Shōnagon|en|Sei Shōnagon|qid=Q231603|lt=Sei Shōnagon|1=Sei Shōnagon}}, and the character of Genji is partially based on Michinaga in the eponymous ''[[:en:Tale of Genji|Tale of Genji]]''.
=== Fujiwara regime in the Heian period === The Fujiwara Regency was the main feature of government during most of the Heian era. {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} ({{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|qid=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}) was geopolitically a better seat of government; with good river access to the sea, it could be reached by land routes from the eastern provinces.
Just before the move to the Heian-kyō, the Emperor had abolished universal conscription in the eighth century and soon local, private militaries came into being. The Fujiwara and the clans of [[Taira clan|Taira]] and [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] created later during the ninth century were among the most prominent families supported by the new military class.
In the ninth and tenth centuries, much authority was lost to the great families, who disregarded the Chinese-style land and tax systems imposed by the government in Kyoto. Stability came to Heian Japan, but, even though succession was ensured for the Imperial family through heredity, power again concentrated in the hands of one noble family, the Fujiwara.
Family administrations now became public institutions. As the most powerful family, the Fujiwara governed Japan and determined the general affairs of state, such as succession to the throne. Family and state affairs were thoroughly intermixed, a pattern followed among other families, monasteries, and even the imperial family.
As the [[Soga clan|Soga]] had taken control of the throne in the sixth century, the Fujiwara by the ninth century had intermarried with the imperial family, and one of their members was the first head of the Emperor's Private Office. While the earliest parts of the Heian period was marked by unusually strong emperors governing themselves (in particular from [[Emperor Kanmu]] to {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|qid=Q316654|lt=Emperor Saga|1=Emperor Saga}} (781–823)), the Fujiwara started to rebuild their influence first under {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|en|Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|qid=Q3090616|lt=Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu|1=Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu}} in the first half of the ninth century. Fuyutsugu's son {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoshifusa|en|Fujiwara no Yoshifusa|qid=Q960841|lt=Fujiwara no Yoshifusa|1=Fujiwara no Yoshifusa}} was the first person not from the imperial family to become regent for a minor emperor when he gained that position when his grandson was enthroned as {{ill|Emperor Seiwa|en|Emperor Seiwa|qid=Q349243|lt=Emperor Seiwa|1=Emperor Seiwa}} in 858. His adopted son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|en|Fujiwara no Mototsune|qid=Q1397196|lt=Fujiwara no Mototsune|1=Fujiwara no Mototsune}}, had himself further appointed ''[[:en:Kampaku|Kampaku]]'' (regent for an adult emperor, a newly invented position). After Mototsune's death {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|Emperor Uda|qid=Q349267|lt=Emperor Uda|1=Emperor Uda}} (who was not the son of a Fujiwara daughter) managed to regain control of much of government. However, after abdicating in favour of his son, {{ill|Emperor Daigo|en|Emperor Daigo|qid=Q329753|lt=Emperor Daigo|1=Emperor Daigo}} (897–930), while apparently intending to control government from retirement, Mototsune's son {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|en|Fujiwara no Tokihira|qid=Q2994735|lt=Fujiwara no Tokihira|1=Fujiwara no Tokihira}} managed to maneuver himself back to very prominent position until his early death in 909. The remaining period of Daigo's reign was again relatively free from Fujiwara dominance, but from the beginning of the reign of his son {{ill|Emperor Suzaku|en|Emperor Suzaku|qid=Q361019|lt=Emperor Suzaku|1=Emperor Suzaku}}, the Fujiwara again re-established their dominance of the court with the leadership of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|en|Fujiwara no Tadahira|qid=Q1397601|lt=Fujiwara no Tadahira|1=Fujiwara no Tadahira}}.
Nevertheless, the Fujiwara were not demoted by Emperor Daigo but in many ways became stronger during his reign. Central control of Japan had continued to decline, and the Fujiwara, along with other great families and religious foundations, acquired ever larger ''[[shōen]]'' and greater wealth during the early tenth century. By the early Heian period, the ''shōen'' had obtained legal status, and the large religious establishments sought clear titles in perpetuity, waiver of taxes, and immunity from government inspection of the ''shōen'' they held. Those people who worked the land found it advantageous to transfer title to shōen holders in return for a share of the harvest. People and lands were increasingly beyond central control and taxation, a de facto return to conditions before the Taika Reform.
(966–1028)]]
Within decades of Emperor Daigo's death, the Fujiwara had absolute control over the court. By the year 1000, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|en|Fujiwara no Michinaga|qid=Q433133|lt=Fujiwara no Michinaga|1=Fujiwara no Michinaga}} was able to enthrone and dethrone emperors at will. Little authority was left for traditional officialdom, and government affairs were handled through the Fujiwara family's private administration. The Fujiwara had become what historian George B. Sansom has called "hereditary dictators".
The Fujiwara presided over a period of cultural and artistic flowering at the imperial court and among the aristocracy. There was great interest in graceful poetry and vernacular literature. Japanese writing had long depended on Chinese ideograms (''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|qid=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}}''), but these were now supplemented by ''{{ill|Kana|en|Kana|qid=Q187659|lt=Kana|1=Kana}}'', two types of phonetic Japanese script: ''{{ill|Katakana|en|Katakana|qid=Q82946|lt=Katakana|1=Katakana}}'', a mnemonic device using parts of Chinese ideograms; and ''{{ill|Hiragana|en|Hiragana|qid=Q48332|lt=Hiragana|1=Hiragana}}'', a cursive form of ''kanji'' writing and an art form in itself. ''Hiragana'' gave written expression to the spoken word and, with it, to the rise in Japan's famous vernacular literature, much of it written by court women who had not been trained in Chinese as had their male counterparts. Three late tenth century and early eleventh century women presented their views of life and romance at the Heian court in ''{{ill|Kagerō Nikki|en|Kagerō Nikki|qid=Q3005729|lt=Kagerō Nikki|1=Kagerō Nikki}}'' ("The Gossamer Years") by "the mother of Michitsuna", ''Makura no Sōshi'' (''{{ill|The Pillow Book|en|The Pillow Book|qid=Q615137|lt=The Pillow Book|1=The Pillow Book}}'') by {{ill|Sei Shōnagon|en|Sei Shōnagon|qid=Q231603|lt=Sei Shōnagon|1=Sei Shōnagon}}, and ''Genji Monogatari'' (''[[:en:Tale of Genji|Tale of Genji]]'') by {{ill|Murasaki Shikibu|en|Murasaki Shikibu|qid=Q81731|lt=Murasaki Shikibu|1=Murasaki Shikibu}} (herself a Fujiwara). Indigenous art also flourished under the Fujiwara after centuries of imitating Chinese forms. Vividly colored ''yamato-e'' (Japanese style) paintings of court life and stories about temples and shrines became common in the mid and late Heian periods, setting patterns for Japanese art to this day.
Decline in food production, growth of the population, and competition for resources among the great families all led to the gradual decline of Fujiwara power and gave rise to military disturbances in the mid-tenth and eleventh centuries. Members of the Fujiwara, [[Taira clan|Taira]], and [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] families—all of whom had descended from the imperial family—attacked one another, claimed control over vast tracts of conquered land, set up rival regimes, and generally broke the peace of Japan.
The Fujiwara controlled the throne until the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Sanjō|en|Emperor Go-Sanjō|qid=Q45844|lt=Emperor Go-Sanjō|1=Emperor Go-Sanjō}} (1068–73), the first emperor not born of a Fujiwara mother since the ninth century. Emperor Go-Sanjō, determined to restore imperial control through strong personal rule, implemented reforms to curb Fujiwara influence. He also established an office to compile and validate [[:en:Estate (house)|estate]] records with the aim of reasserting central control. Many ''[[shōen]]'' were not properly certified, and large landholders, like the Fujiwara, felt threatened with the loss of their lands. Emperor Go-Sanjō also established the ''In no chō'', or Office of the Cloistered Emperor, which was held by a succession of emperors who abdicated to devote themselves to behind-the-scenes governance, or ''insei'' ({{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|qid=Q865416|lt=Cloistered rule|1=Cloistered rule}}).
The ''In no chō'' filled the void left by the decline of Fujiwara power. Rather than being banished, the Fujiwara were mostly retained in their old positions of civil dictator and minister of the center while being bypassed in decision making. In time, many of the Fujiwara were replaced, mostly by members of the rising Minamoto family. While the Fujiwara fell into disputes among themselves and formed northern and southern factions, the insei system allowed the paternal line of the imperial family to gain influence over the throne. The period from 1086 to 1156 was the age of supremacy of the ''In no chō'' and of the rise of the military class throughout the country. Military might rather than civil authority dominated the government.
A struggle for succession in the mid-twelfth century gave the Fujiwara an opportunity to regain their former power. [[Fujiwara no Yorinaga]] sided with the retired emperor in a violent battle in 1158 against the heir apparent, who was supported by the Taira and Minamoto. In the end, the Fujiwara were destroyed, the old system of government supplanted, and the ''insei'' system left powerless as bushi took control of court affairs, marking a turning point in Japanese history. Within a year, the Taira and Minamoto clashed, and a twenty-year period of Taira ascendancy began. The Taira were seduced by court life and ignored problems in the provinces. Finally, [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–99) rose from his headquarters at {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|qid=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}} (in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|qid=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}}, southwest of modern Tokyo) to defeat the Taira, and with them the child emperor {{ill|Emperor Antoku|en|Emperor Antoku|qid=Q316651|lt=Emperor Antoku|1=Emperor Antoku}} they controlled, in the {{ill|Genpei War|en|Genpei War|qid=Q737542|lt=Genpei War|1=Genpei War}} (1180–85).
After this downfall, the younger branches of the Fujiwara clan turned their focus from politics to the arts, producing literary scholars including {{ill|Fujiwara no Shunzei|en|Fujiwara no Shunzei|qid=Q934255|lt=Fujiwara no Shunzei|1=Fujiwara no Shunzei}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|en|Fujiwara no Teika|qid=Q378425|lt=Fujiwara no Teika|1=Fujiwara no Teika}}.
=== Decline === Only forty years after [[:en:Michinaga|Michinaga]]'s death, his Fujiwara heirs were not able to prevent the accession of [[:en:Emperor Go-Sanjo|Emperor Go-Sanjō]] (reigned 1068–73), the first emperor since {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|Emperor Uda|qid=Q349267|lt=Emperor Uda|1=Emperor Uda}} whose mother was not a Fujiwara. The system of government by retired emperor (''daijō tennō'') ({{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|qid=Q865416|lt=Cloistered rule|1=Cloistered rule}}) beginning from 1087 further weakened the Fujiwara's control over the Imperial Court.
The Fujiwara-dominated Heian period approached its end along disturbances of 12th century. The dynastic struggle known as the Hōgen Disturbance (''[[:en:Hōgen Rebellion|Hōgen no Ran]]'') led to the Taira emerging as the most powerful clan in 1156. During the Heiji Disturbance (''{{ill|Heiji rebellion|en|Heiji rebellion|qid=Q944859|lt=Heiji no Ran|1=Heiji rebellion}}'') in 1160 the Taira defeated the coalition of Fujiwara and Minamoto forces. This defeat marked the end of the Fujiwara's dominance.
== Split and enduring influence == During the 13th century, the {{ill|Fujiwara Hok-ke|en|Fujiwara Hok-ke|qid=Q2975268|lt=Fujiwara Hok-ke|1=Fujiwara Hok-ke}} was split into {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|qid=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses|1=Five regent houses}}: {{ill|Konoe family|en|Konoe family|qid=Q1781840|lt=Konoe|1=Konoe family}}, {{ill|Takatsukasa family|en|Takatsukasa family|qid=Q846799|lt=Takatsukasa|1=Takatsukasa family}}, {{ill|Kujō family|en|Kujō family|qid=Q1791350|lt=Kujō|1=Kujō family}}, [[:en:Nijo family|Nijō]] and {{ill|Ichijō family|en|Ichijō family|qid=Q1535498|lt=Ichijō|1=Ichijō family}}.
They had a "monopoly" to the offices of ''sesshō'' and ''kampaku'', and served in turn. The political power had shifted away from the court nobility in Kyoto to the new {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=warrior class|1=Samurai}} in the countryside. However, Fujiwara remained close advisers, regents and ministers to the emperors for centuries; the family retained political reputation and influence even until the 20th century (such as {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|qid=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe|1=Fumimaro Konoe}} and {{ill|Morihiro Hosokawa|en|Morihiro Hosokawa|qid=Q315555|lt=Morihiro Hosokawa|1=Morihiro Hosokawa}}, who became the Prime Ministers). As such, they had a certain political power and much influence, as often the rival warriors and later bakufu sought their alliance. {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|qid=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} and his sister {{ill|Oichi|en|Oichi|qid=Q635214|lt=Oichi|1=Oichi}} claimed to have descent from the [[Taira clan|Taira]] and Fujiwara clans; regent {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} and shogun {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} were related by marriage to various families from Fujiwara clan. {{ill|Empress Shōken|en|Empress Shōken|qid=Q269520|lt=Empress Shōken|1=Empress Shōken}}, wife of {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}}, was a descendant of the Fujiwara clan.
Until the marriage of the Crown Prince {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|qid=Q34479|lt=Hirohito|1=Hirohito}} (Emperor Shōwa) to Princess Nagako of Kuni (posthumously {{ill|Empress Nagako|en|Empress Nagako|qid=Q230697|lt=Empress Nagako|1=Empress Nagako}}) in January 1924, the principal consorts of emperors and crown princes had often been recruited from one of the [[:en:Sekke|Sekke]] Fujiwara. Imperial princesses were often married to Fujiwara lords – throughout a millennium at least. As recently as Emperor Shōwa's third daughter, the former {{ill|Kazuko Takatsukasa|en|Kazuko Takatsukasa|qid=Q1030373|lt=Princess Kazuko|1=Kazuko Takatsukasa}} and {{ill|Takahito, Prince Mikasa|en|Takahito, Prince Mikasa|qid=Q280229|lt=Prince Mikasa's|1=Takahito, Prince Mikasa}} elder daughter, the former {{ill|Yasuko Konoe|en|Yasuko Konoe|qid=Q7137456|lt=Princess Yasuko|1=Yasuko Konoe}}, married into Takatsukasa and Konoe families, respectively. Likewise a daughter of the last [[Tokugawa shogunate|''shōgun'']] married a second cousin of Emperor Shōwa.
==Family tree== {{main|Fujiwara family tree}} {{Fujiwara family tree}}
